era_context: The early-to-mid 8th century CE is a period of dynastic consolidation and religious transformation. The Tang Dynasty dominates East Asia; the Umayyad Caliphate is entering a period of decline; the Byzantine Empire is consolidating iconoclastic policies; and the Frankish Kingdom is preparing for the Carolingian transition. This is a transitional decade before major upheavals that would reshape Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (trade routes documented but local political events poorly attested)

events.title: Constantine V becomes Byzantine Emperor
events.description: Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) becomes Byzantine Emperor following the death of his father Leo III. Constantine V is known for his military prowess and his aggressive enforcement of iconoclasm (the destruction and prohibition of religious images). His accession marks the beginning of a period of intense religious conflict within the Byzantine Empire. Constantine V would become one of the most controversial figures in Byzantine history, both celebrated for his military victories and condemned by icon-venerating Christians for his religious policies.
key_figures: Constantine V Copronymus (Byzantine Emperor, r. 741–775 CE)

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronographia, compiled early 9th century
Nikephoros I, Patriarch of Constantinople, Breviarium, 9th century

events.certainty_note: Constantine V's accession in 741 CE (or possibly 742 CE; sources vary slightly) is well-attested in Byzantine historiography. The date is confirmed in multiple sources, though there is minor uncertainty about whether his reign began in 741 or 742 CE.
cross_references: 726-001 (Beginning of Iconoclastic Controversy under Leo III)

events.title: Frankish military consolidation under Pepin III
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), mayor of the palace and de facto ruler of the Frankish Kingdom, continues military campaigns to consolidate Frankish power. In 742 CE, Frankish forces are active in various theaters, including campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other neighboring territories. These campaigns are part of the broader Carolingian expansion that would culminate in Pepin's deposition of the last Merovingian king (751 CE).
key_figures: Pepin III (the Short, Mayor of the Palace)
Childeric III (Last Merovingian King, nominal authority)

sources.name: Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled 9th century
Liber Historiae Francorum (Book of Frankish History), 8th century

events.certainty_note: Frankish military activity in this period is attested in Carolingian annals, though specific campaigns in 742 CE are sometimes conflated with adjacent years.

events.title: Umayyad Caliphate under Hisham II; internal instability
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Damascus and wider Islamic world)
events.description: Hisham II (r. 724–743 CE) rules the Umayyad Caliphate during a period of increasing internal instability. His reign is marked by factional conflicts, provincial unrest, and the growing power of non-Arab (mawali) populations who resent Arab privilege. The caliphate is fragmenting, particularly in Iberia and North Africa, where Berber and Arab factions are competing for power. Hisham II's death in 743 CE would lead to the succession of Walid II and further instability.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 10th century
Ibn al-Athir, Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, compiled 13th century

events.certainty_note: Hisham II's reign and the Umayyad Caliphate's internal instability are well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date of his death in 743 CE is confirmed in multiple sources.

events.title: Tang Dynasty administrative and cultural flourishing
events.description: The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong continues its period of cultural and administrative flourishing. The 740s CE represent the height of Tang power and influence, with extensive trade networks (including the Silk Road), military dominance in East and Central Asia, and cultural prestige. Poetry, painting, and other arts flourish at the Tang court. This stability and prosperity would be disrupted by the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE).

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 10th century
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's cultural and administrative activities in the 740s CE are well-attested in Chinese historiography, though specific events in 742 CE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.

events.title: Abbasid movement organizing in Khorasan and Central Asia
events.description: The Abbasid movement, a revolutionary Shi'a-influenced faction opposed to Umayyad rule, continues its organizational work in Khorasan and Central Asia. By 742 CE, the Abbasid da'wa (missionary organization) is well-established among disaffected Persian, Khorasani, and Shi'a populations. This organizational work, though not yet openly revolutionary, is laying the groundwork for the Abbasid Revolution that would formally begin in 747 CE and succeed by 750 CE.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 10th century

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid movement's organizational phase is attested in later Islamic historiography, but contemporary documentation is minimal. The exact timeline of the da'wa's development is reconstructed from later sources and is subject to scholarly debate.

disconfirming_evidence: Constantine V's accession date is slightly uncertain; sources vary between 741 and 742 CE. The exact year of his accession is debated by modern scholars. Hisham II's reign and the Umayyad Caliphate's internal instability are well-attested, but the extent to which these factors directly caused the caliphate's collapse (as opposed to being one factor among many) is debated. Frankish campaigns in 742 CE are attested only in general terms; specific military engagements are not documented. The Abbasid movement's organizational work in 742 CE is inferred from later sources and is not directly attested in contemporary documentation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 742 CE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Islamic sources (compiled in the 10th–13th centuries) provide narrative frameworks for Umayyad and Abbasid history, though contemporary documentation is sparse. Byzantine sources exist but are often polemical regarding iconoclasm. Frankish sources are sparse and compiled centuries later. Chinese sources are relatively rich but focus on court and administrative matters. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are almost entirely absent from contemporary documentation. The surviving sources are predominantly elite-focused and reflect the biases of later compilers. Archaeological evidence for this year is limited.

graph_edges.note: Constantine V's accession in 742 CE begins his reign, which lasts until 775 CE
Hisham II's death in 743 CE leads to Walid II's accession
Abbasid organizational work in 742 CE leads to formal revolution in 747 CE
